questions.stem: An 18-year-old male rugby player presents to ED after a tackle. He has severe right shoulder pain and is holding his arm slightly abducted and externally rotated. On examination, there is loss of the deltoid contour and a palpable fullness anteriorly. Sensation over the regimental badge area is intact and distal pulses are present. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The arm held in external rotation, loss of deltoid contour, and anterior fullness are classic for anterior shoulder dislocation. Posterior dislocation would show internal rotation and posterior fullness. ACJ dislocation has a step at the acromioclavicular joint. Proximal humerus fracture often presents with crepitus. Rotator cuff tear typically causes weakness without the classic deformity.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents with recurrent episodes of her left shoulder 'popping out' during overhead activities. She had a first-time anterior dislocation two years ago which was reduced in ED. Since then, she has experienced multiple subluxations, especially when serving in tennis. She has no other joint laxity. X-rays are normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The history of prior traumatic dislocation followed by recurrent subluxations in the same direction (overhead) is typical of recurrent anterior instability. Multidirectional instability usually involves bilateral symptoms and generalised ligamentous laxity. SLAP tear often presents with clicking or locking. Impingement shows a painful arc. Arthritis is unlikely at this age.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man is brought to ED after a generalised tonic-clonic seizure. He complains of right shoulder pain. Examination reveals his arm is held adducted and internally rotated, with a prominent posterior bulge. There is no anterior fullness. Axillary nerve sensation is intact. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The mechanism (seizure) and internal rotation with posterior prominence are classic for posterior shoulder dislocation, which is often missed. Anterior dislocation would show external rotation and anterior fullness. Biceps rupture has a Popeye deformity. Glenoid fracture has crepitus and limited motion. Adhesive capsulitis is gradual and not acute.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman presents to clinic with a 6-month history of progressive right shoulder stiffness and pain, which started after she sustained a fall on her outstretched hand. She was diagnosed with a shoulder dislocation at the time and had a successful closed reduction in ED. However, she never regained full range of motion and now has marked restriction of both active and passive movements in all planes. X-rays show preserved joint space and no fracture. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Global restriction of both active and passive motion following a period of immobilisation after dislocation is classic for adhesive capsulitis. Rotator cuff tear would allow passive motion but weakness. Osteoarthritis would show joint space narrowing on X-ray. Recurrent dislocation presents with instability, not stiffness. Axillary nerve palsy would cause deltoid weakness and sensory loss.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old basketball player presents with a first-time shoulder dislocation while reaching for a rebound. In ED, the shoulder is reduced successfully. However, after reduction, he has persistent numbness over the lateral aspect of his shoulder and weakness in arm abduction. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Axillary nerve injury complicating shoulder dislocation
questions.explanation: Numbness over the regimental badge area and weakness of deltoid are pathognomonic for axillary nerve injury, the most common nerve injury in anterior dislocation. Brachial plexus injury affects multiple dermatomes. Supraspinatus rupture causes pain on empty can test without sensory loss. Posterior dislocation would have different limb positioning. Rotator cuff tear generally presents with weakness and pain, not numbness.

questions.stem: A 20-year-old male presents to the emergency department after a rugby tackle. He has severe right shoulder pain, holds his arm in external rotation, and the shoulder appears ‘squared-off’. Which single investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: In suspected anterior shoulder dislocation, standard AP and lateral (or scapular Y) X-rays confirm the diagnosis and exclude associated fractures. Axillary view is less urgent and can be difficult in acute pain. CT, ultrasound, and MRI are not first-line.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old builder falls from a ladder and lands on his right shoulder. On examination, the shoulder appears ‘squared-off’ and the radial pulse is absent. What is the single most appropriate initial investigation?
options: CT angiogram of the upper limb
Doppler ultrasound of the axillary artery
questions.explanation: In suspected dislocation with vascular compromise, an immediate X-ray confirms dislocation and identifies fractures, guiding urgent reduction. Although vascular imaging is important, it follows reduction and X-ray. The priority is to confirm the dislocation.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old basketball player has had three episodes of anterior shoulder dislocation after minimal trauma. An X-ray after reduction is normal. Which investigation is most appropriate to identify the underlying soft-tissue lesion?
questions.explanation: Recurrent instability in a young patient is often due to a Bankart (labral) lesion. MR arthrography has the highest sensitivity for labral tears, superior to plain MRI. CT arthrogram is an alternative but involves radiation and less contrast resolution for labrum.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old woman presents after a fall onto her outstretched left hand. She complains of shoulder pain and cannot externally rotate her arm. An AP shoulder X-ray appears normal. What is the best next investigation to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Posterior dislocation is often missed on AP X-ray. An axillary view best demonstrates the humeral head posterior to the glenoid. CT is also diagnostic but involves more radiation and is not first-line if axillary view can be obtained. Scapular Y view may show the dislocation but axillary is more definitive.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man had an anterior shoulder dislocation reduced in the emergency department. A post-reduction AP X-ray is reviewed. Which of the following radiographic findings suggests a Hill-Sachs lesion?
options: A triangular fragment off the anteroinferior glenoid
A notch on the posterolateral humeral head
A double density overlying the glenoid
A trough sign on the anteromedial humeral head
questions.explanation: Hill-Sachs lesion is an impaction fracture of the posterolateral humeral head from the glenoid rim during anterior dislocation. The trough sign is associated with posterior dislocation (reverse Hill-Sachs). The triangular fragment is a bony Bankart. Lightbulb sign suggests posterior dislocation.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old rugby player presents to the Emergency Department with a right shoulder deformity following a tackle. He has no previous shoulder problems. X-ray confirms an anterior dislocation of the glenohumeral joint. There is no neurovascular deficit. What is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Physiotherapy with early range of motion exercises
Closed reduction with intravenous sedation and analgesia
Immobilisation in a sling for 4 weeks without reduction
questions.explanation: For a first-time traumatic anterior dislocation without complications, closed reduction under sedation is first-line. Open reduction is reserved for irreducible dislocations or fractures. Immobilisation is post-reduction, not definitive. Surgery is for recurrent instability.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman has experienced three episodes of anterior shoulder dislocation in the past year, each occurring with minimal trauma. MRI confirms a Bankart lesion. She is otherwise well. What is the most appropriate definitive treatment to prevent further dislocations?
options: Immobilisation in a shoulder immobiliser for 6 weeks
Continued physiotherapy with rotator cuff strengthening
questions.explanation: For recurrent traumatic instability with a Bankart lesion, arthroscopic Bankart repair is first-line surgical treatment. Latarjet is for failed soft-tissue repair or glenoid bone loss. Physiotherapy alone is insufficient for trauma-induced instability. Immobilisation is acute management.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man presents with an anterior shoulder dislocation after a fall. Examination reveals a 'square' shoulder deformity and inability to abduct the shoulder due to deltoid weakness. Sensation over the 'regimental badge' area is reduced. There is no vascular compromise. What is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Immobilisation and referral to orthopaedics for MRI in 1 week
Urgent nerve conduction studies prior to any reduction attempt
Application of a sling and analgesia, with outpatient physiotherapy
Emergency closed reduction under sedation followed by reassessment of nerve function
Open reduction and immediate nerve exploration
questions.explanation: Axillary nerve palsy is common in anterior dislocation. Closed reduction should be performed first, then nerve function reassessed. Most recover spontaneously. Nerve exploration is rarely needed acutely. Delaying reduction risks more injury.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old woman with osteoporosis presents after a fall on outstretched hand. X-ray shows an anterior shoulder dislocation. After successful closed reduction, she remains unable to actively abduct the arm to 90°, and has a painful arc. An ultrasound reveals a full-thickness supraspinatus tear. What is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Open rotator cuff repair with tendon transfer
questions.explanation: In elderly patients, full-thickness rotator cuff tears are often degenerative and may be managed conservatively with physiotherapy, especially if acute from dislocation. Surgical repair has higher failure rates in this age group. Steroid injection is for symptoms but not definitive treatment of the tear.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old male epileptic patient is brought to the Emergency Department after a tonic-clonic seizure. He complains of left shoulder pain and cannot externally rotate his arm. X-ray shows a posterior dislocation of the glenohumeral joint. There is no neurovascular deficit. What is the definitive management?
options: Attempt closed reduction using gentle traction and internal rotation
Immediate open reduction due to the risk of humeral head fracture
Attempt closed reduction using traction and external rotation
Immobilise in internal rotation and refer for surgery
MRI scan before any reduction attempt
questions.explanation: For posterior dislocation, reduction is achieved by in-line traction, adduction, and gentle external rotation to unlock the humeral head from the posterior glenoid. Internal rotation would worsen the dislocation. Open reduction is not first-line. MRI is unnecessary if the X-ray is diagnostic.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department after a rugby tackle with left shoulder pain and loss of normal deltoid contour. X-ray confirms an anterior glenohumeral dislocation. Closed reduction is performed successfully, and a post-reduction X-ray shows concentric reduction. There is normal sensation over the deltoid and the patient can move the fingers. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Arrange an MRI scan of the shoulder
Discharge with oral analgesia and advice
Apply a collar and cuff sling
Check pulse and capillary refill of the affected limb
questions.explanation: The immediate priority after reduction is to confirm intact neurovascular status. Although the patient has normal sensation, a thorough vascular check (pulse, capillary refill) is essential to exclude axillary artery injury before applying a sling and discharging.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with a history of three previous anterior shoulder dislocations presents to the Emergency Department after her shoulder dislocated while reaching overhead. Closed reduction is performed easily, and post-reduction X-ray is satisfactory. She has no neurovascular deficit. What is the most appropriate immediate next step in her management?
options: Discharge with a sling and advise rest for 6 weeks
Refer to a specialist shoulder clinic for consideration of stabilization surgery
Prescribe a course of oral corticosteroids
questions.explanation: Recurrent dislocations in a young patient are likely due to a Bankart lesion requiring surgical stabilization. The immediate next step is referral to a shoulder specialist; prolonged immobilization or corticosteroids are not indicated, and physiotherapy should only begin after specialist assessment.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department after a fall onto her outstretched hand, with right shoulder pain and deformity. X-ray shows an anterior dislocation with a greater tuberosity fracture. Closed reduction is attempted in the ED but fails due to muscle spasm. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Apply a sling and refer to fracture clinic
Arrange a CT scan of the shoulder
Repeat the reduction attempt under intravenous sedation
Request an orthopaedic review for reduction in the operating theatre
questions.explanation: A failed closed reduction of a fracture-dislocation in an elderly patient often requires general anaesthesia for muscle relaxation. The most appropriate immediate next step is to escalate to the orthopaedic team for reduction in theatre; repeating attempts without adequate relaxation risks further injury.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with severe right shoulder pain after a tonic-clonic seizure. Examination reveals flattening of the anterior shoulder and a prominent posterior bulge. X-ray shows a 'light bulb' sign of the humeral head. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Request an MRI to confirm the diagnosis
Refer to orthopaedics for urgent reduction
Apply a collar and cuff sling and discharge
Perform gentle rotational reduction under sedation
questions.explanation: The X-ray findings indicate a posterior dislocation, which is often missed and difficult to reduce. Referral to orthopaedics for reduction (often under sedation or anaesthesia) is essential; blind attempts in the ED carry a risk of fracture or iatrogenic injury.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with left shoulder dislocation after a fall. X-ray confirms an anterior dislocation. Closed reduction is performed successfully with concentric reduction on post-reduction X-ray. On examination, there is reduced sensation over the lateral aspect of the shoulder and the patient cannot abduct the arm beyond 30 degrees. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer to orthopaedics for immediate nerve exploration
Apply a collar and cuff sling and advise follow-up in shoulder clinic
Start high-dose oral prednisolone for nerve palsy
Request an urgent nerve conduction study
Perform a shoulder ultrasound to assess for rotator cuff tear
questions.explanation: The findings suggest an isolated axillary nerve neuropraxia, which is common after dislocation and typically recovers spontaneously. The immediate management is to support the shoulder with a sling and arrange outpatient follow-up; nerve conduction studies, exploration, or steroids are not indicated acutely.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man presents to the emergency department after a rugby tackle, with a left shoulder 'popped out'. The dislocation is reduced under sedation in the ED. Post-reduction, he complains of persistent numbness over the lateral aspect of his shoulder. What clinical finding is most indicative of an axillary nerve injury?
options: Numbness over the regimental badge area (lateral shoulder)
Loss of sensation over the thenar eminence
questions.explanation: The axillary nerve supplies the deltoid muscle and the skin over the lateral shoulder ('regimental badge' area). This is the most common neurological injury in anterior shoulder dislocation. Distractors relate to other peripheral nerves: little finger (ulnar), finger abduction (ulnar), thenar (median), elbow flexion (musculocutaneous).

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with osteoporosis presents after a fall onto her right shoulder. X-ray shows an anterior shoulder dislocation. There is a separate bony fragment seen near the humeral head. What finding on the X-ray necessitates urgent orthopaedic consultation?
options: Bony Bankart lesion of the glenoid
Comminuted fracture of the humeral head
questions.explanation: A displaced greater tuberosity fracture is an important associated injury in elderly patients with dislocation. It often requires surgical fixation due to rotator cuff involvement and risk of non-union. While Hill-Sachs and Bankart lesions are common, they do not mandate urgent surgical referral. A comminuted humeral head fracture is rare with simple dislocation and would be a separate injury.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with a history of recurrent shoulder dislocations presents with acute-onset shoulder pain after an epileptic seizure. His arm is held in internal rotation and he cannot externally rotate it actively or passively. What clinical sign most strongly suggests the diagnosis?
options: Fixed internal rotation deformity with inability to externally rotate
Palpable humeral head anteriorly under the coracoid
Prominent acromion and loss of deltoid contour
Loss of normal shoulder contour (square shoulder)
questions.explanation: Posterior dislocation is commonly missed, especially in seizure patients. The cardinal sign is a fixed internal rotation deformity with inability to externally rotate. Other options: sulcus sign indicates inferior instability; square shoulder and palpable anterior head are typical of anterior dislocation (which would allow external rotation).

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man is brought to the emergency department with an anterior shoulder dislocation after a fall. After gentle closed reduction in the ED, the radial pulse remains absent on the affected side. What does this finding indicate?
options: Compartment syndrome of the upper arm
questions.explanation: An absent radial pulse after reduction is a red flag for axillary artery injury (often thrombosis or intimal tear). This is a limb-threatening emergency requiring urgent vascular surgical assessment. Vasospasm is possible but should not be assumed—persistent absence mandates investigation. Brachial plexus injury would cause neurological, not vascular, signs. Vein thrombosis does not cause pulselessness.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with right shoulder pain after a fall. She is able to move the arm but with limited external rotation. An anteroposterior X-ray of the shoulder shows the humeral head appearing symmetrically round, like a 'light bulb'. What does this radiographic sign suggest?
questions.explanation: The 'light bulb sign' on AP X-ray is classic for posterior shoulder dislocation. In this view, the humeral head appears symmetrical and rounded because it is fixed in internal rotation. Anterior dislocation shows the humeral head overlapping the glenoid; inferior dislocation appears with arm abducted. A fracture would show cortical disruption.
